runs.interpretation: Intensive-margin uncompensated annual-hours elasticity: the percent change in annual hours worked per 1% change in the net-of-tax wage (including substitution and income effects) for prime-age U.S. workers already employed, excluding participation changes.
citations: Standard labor supply theory (Slutsky decomposition; expected sign and size for uncompensated intensive elasticities)
Empirical labor supply patterns from U.S. micro evidence on hours responses to net wage changes (often modest negative uncompensated intensive elasticities)

runs.reasoningSummary: For prime-age workers already working, compensated/substitution effects are often modest, while income effects tend to offset in a way that typically yields an uncompensated intensive-hours elasticity that is slightly negative with limited magnitude; I center the belief near -0.2 with a plausible range from roughly -0.6 to near zero.

runs.interpretation: Intensive-margin (already-working) uncompensated annual-hours elasticity: the percent change in annual hours worked from a 1% change in the net-of-tax wage, including substitution and income effects (excluding labor-force participation changes).
citations: Standard labor-supply intuition with offsetting substitution and income effects on hours at the intensive margin
Empirical wage–hours elasticities from reduced-form labor-supply studies often near small positive values for prime-age workers
Theoretical predictions (e.g., Slutsky-style decomposition) implying limited net uncompensated responses when income effects partially offset substitution
runs.reasoningSummary: For prime-age workers already employed, the uncompensated wage effect on hours is typically modest: substitution tends to raise hours with higher net wages, while income effects partially counteract. I therefore place the median slightly above 0 and allow a small chance of near-zero or mildly negative responses, with an upper tail reflecting stronger responsiveness for some workers/groups.

runs.interpretation: Intensive-margin uncompensated annual-hours elasticity of labor supply: the percent change in annual hours worked for a 1% change in the net-of-tax wage, holding participation fixed (excluding labor-force entry/exit) and allowing both substitution and income effects.
citations: Standard labor-supply micro evidence (Altonji & Paxson-style) suggesting small uncompensated intensive-margin elasticities for prime-age workers
Textbook labor supply framework (Slutsky decomposition) where uncompensated elasticity often exceeds compensated only modestly for hours
Empirical summaries from discrete-choice/structural labor supply literatures indicating substantial heterogeneity across workers but generally modest median responses
runs.reasoningSummary: For prime-age workers already employed, I expect the net-of-tax wage to induce a relatively small positive response in annual hours once participation is excluded; income and substitution effects partially offset, but uncompensated elasticities are still typically positive and modest in magnitude for a median worker, with a wide upper tail reflecting heterogeneity.
